title: NIS2 Cyber Hygiene Practices — Baseline Control Compliance
exclusions: Does not approve compensating controls for offline OT assets, replace field audits of physical kiosks, or prove phishing simulation results (see UC-22.2.10) although both topics relate to hygiene.
evidence: `UC-22.2.28` CSV export with `asset_fqdn` and failure bitmask, underlying `tenable:sc:assets` event samples for contested hosts, and timestamp token from `evidenceSigning` retained alongside quarterly hygiene steering minutes.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(g) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(g) (Cyber-hygiene and training) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.28: NIS2 Cyber Hygiene Practices — Baseline Control Compliance.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.28' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: QA tenant drops five servers from EDR policy while lookup still marks them `business_service=customer_billing`; nightly run shows `hygiene_failures>=1` for each host and totals five rows on the fail dashboard tile.
controlTest.negativeScenario: After EDR agent reinstall, Tenable `plugins_text` regains vendor string, expectations CSV removes decommissioned assets, and seven-day rolling run returns zero failures while spot-check in **Tenable.sc → Asset lists → Compliance** remains green.

dataSources: `index=tenable` `sourcetype=tenable:sc:assets` (fields dns_name, plugins_text, uuid) via Tenable Add-On for Splunk ([Splunkbase 4060](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4060)); `index=windows` `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security` for EventCode 4624 MFA claims when integrated with Windows Hello; `index=auth` `sourcetype=okta:log` from Splunk Add-on for Okta ([Splunkbase 6553](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/6553)); lookup `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv` listing `asset_fqdn`, `business_service`, mandatory controls.
app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (Splunkbase 4055)
spl:
| inputlookup nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv
| join asset_fqdn [
    search index=tenable sourcetype="tenable:sc:assets" earliest=-1d
    | eval mfa_ok=if(match(plugins_text,"(?i)mfa|okta|hello|yubi"),1,0)
    | eval edr_ok=if(match(plugins_text,"(?i)edr|defender|crowdstrike|sentinel"),1,0)
    | stats latest(mfa_ok) as mfa_ok latest(edr_ok) as edr_ok by dns_name
    | rename dns_name as asset_fqdn
]
| eval hygiene_failures=if(mfa_ok==0,1,0) + if(edr_ok==0,1,0)
| where hygiene_failures > 0
| table asset_fqdn, business_service, mfa_ok, edr_ok, hygiene_failures
description: Scores each in-scope row from `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv` against Tenable.sc asset export fields to reveal missing multi-factor markers or endpoint protection plugins so hygiene backlogs surface before auditors ask for screenshots.
value: Cyber hygiene is not a single patch Tuesday—it is an ongoing assurance that baseline protections exist on every system supporting essential services; rolling that proof into Splunk prevents the last-minute scramble of logging into Tenable manually for each subsidiary.
implementation: (1) Keep `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv` aligned with CMDB owners. (2) Schedule Tenable import hourly. (3) Weekly remediation campaign emails via ServiceNow. (4) Escalate chronic failures to service director. (5) Board deck uses summarized `hygiene_failures`. (6) Sign exports with `evidenceSigning`.
visualization: Treemap by business_service, bar of failure counts, single-value fleet compliance.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.dest, Vulnerabilities.severity | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable for Vulnerabilities if merging plugin CVE context

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud

knownFalsePositives: 1) **Tenable agent offline >48h** so `plugins_text` stale lacks newest EDR string. *Distinguish:* `last_seen` epoch inside asset JSON. *Suppress:* `where last_seen>relative_time(now(),"-2d")`. 2) **Linux servers exempt from Windows Hello MFA** flagged by regex expecting Windows-only tokens. *Distinguish:* `business_service` tagged `linux_batch`. *Suppress:* lookup `nis2_os_exception.csv`. 3) **Containers sharing DNS with host** collide in join. *Distinguish:* asset type field from CMDB. *Suppress:* filter `where asset_type!="container"`. 4) **Okta-only shops** where Windows events never show MFA text. *Distinguish:* `mfa_ok` sourced from `index=auth`. *Suppress:* append alternate subsearch `OR [ search index=auth ...]`. 5) **Merger integration duplicates** FQDN uppercase vs lowercase. *Distinguish:* case mismatch. *Suppress:* `eval asset_fqdn=lower(asset_fqdn)` pre-join.
equipmentModels: CrowdStrike Falcon (EDR presence detection via Tenable plugin text matching)
Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (EDR presence detection via Tenable plugin text matching)
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742, EventCode 4624 MFA claims)
Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553, MFA enrollment evidence)
Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060, Tenable.sc asset export with plugins_text)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• This UC implements NIS2 Art.21(2)(g) — basic cyber hygiene practices. Art.21(2)(g) requires entities to maintain "basic cyber-hygiene practices" including regular software updates, secure configuration, network segmentation, identity and access management, and user awareness. This UC focuses on two specific hygiene controls that can be verified through telemetry: (1) MFA presence — does the asset show evidence of multi-factor authentication integration, and (2) EDR presence — does the asset have endpoint detection and response software installed. These are the two most impactful baseline controls an organisation can deploy, and their absence on any NIS2-scoped asset is a direct audit finding.
• Tenable.sc ≥6.2 (or Tenable.io) with the Splunk Add-on for Tenable (Splunkbase 4060) configured to export asset data to `index=tenable sourcetype=tenable:sc:assets`. The critical field is `plugins_text` — a concatenated text blob of all Tenable plugin output for the asset. This field contains strings like "CrowdStrike Falcon Sensor detected" or "Okta Verify agent installed" or "Microsoft Defender Advanced Threat Protection running." The regex matching in the SPL searches this text for evidence of MFA and EDR tools. The `plugins_text` field is large (often 10-50 KB per asset) and contributes significantly to index volume — budget approximately 20 KB per asset per export cycle.
• Understanding the `plugins_text` detection approach: this is a heuristic, not a definitive check. Tenable scans detect installed software by examining running processes, registry keys, installed packages, and service configurations. The `plugins_text` contains the combined output of all plugins that ran against the asset. If a plugin detected CrowdStrike, the text will contain "CrowdStrike" somewhere. The regex `(?i)edr|defender|crowdstrike|sentinel` casts a wide net to catch various EDR products. False positives are possible (a document containing "defender" in its title might match), but false negatives are more concerning (an EDR agent installed but not detected by any Tenable plugin). The approach trades precision for coverage — acceptable for a hygiene compliance check but not for security posture assessment.
• Lookup `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv` with columns: `asset_fqdn` (the fully qualified domain name matching Tenable's `dns_name` field), `business_service` (the NIS2-scoped service), `mandatory_mfa` (boolean: 1 if MFA is required for this asset, 0 if exempt — e.g., air-gapped OT systems), `mandatory_edr` (boolean: 1 if EDR is required, 0 if exempt — e.g., legacy systems where EDR agents can't run). The `mandatory_*` columns implement proportionality: not every asset needs every control. Store in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-hygiene/lookups/` and version-control in Git. The CISO office owns this lookup and updates it quarterly based on the asset register.
• Optional: lookup `nis2_edr_markers.csv` with columns: `product_name` (e.g., "CrowdStrike Falcon", "Microsoft Defender", "SentinelOne", "Carbon Black"), `regex_pattern` (the regex to match in `plugins_text`). This externalises the EDR detection patterns from the SPL, making it easier to add new EDR products without modifying the saved search. Similarly, `nis2_mfa_markers.csv` with MFA product patterns.
• Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553) — optional but recommended for SaaS-only MFA verification. If your MFA is provided by Okta (not detected via Tenable plugins), you can supplement the `mfa_ok` check with Okta factor enrollment data from `index=auth sourcetype=okta:log`.
• Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742) — optional for Windows Hello for Business MFA claims. EventCode 4624 with `AuthenticationPackageName=Negotiate` and `KeyLength=0` (or `ImpersonationLevel=%%1833`) can indicate MFA-backed logon in Windows Hello environments.
• Index retention: `index=tenable` ≥ 30 days (the SPL uses `earliest=-1d` for current state, but trend analysis benefits from 30 days).
• RBAC: role `nis2_hygiene_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = tenable, auth, windows, audit_evidence`.
• License estimate: Tenable.sc asset exports at 20 KB per asset, hourly, for 25,000 assets = approximately 12 GB/day. Consider reducing the export interval to every 6 hours (`21600` seconds) unless real-time hygiene posture is required.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**Tenable.sc asset export:**
Install the Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060). Navigate to Apps > Tenable Add-On > Configuration > Account. Provide:
- API URL: `https://tenablesc.example.com` (or `https://cloud.tenable.com` for Tenable.io)
- Access Key and Secret Key (for Tenable.io) or Username/Password (for Tenable.sc)
- Enable the "Asset" input with interval `900` seconds (15 minutes for near-real-time) or `3600` seconds (hourly for lower volume)
- Index: `tenable`
- Sourcetype: `tenable:sc:assets`

Verify the feed:
```
index=tenable sourcetype=tenable:sc:assets earliest=-24h
| stats count dc(dns_name) as assets
| table count assets
```
Expected: `assets` should approximate your total scanned asset count. If `count=0`, check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd source="*tenable*" ERROR`.

**Field verification — `plugins_text` availability:**
```
index=tenable sourcetype=tenable:sc:assets earliest=-1d
| head 5
| eval pt_len=len(plugins_text)
| table dns_name uuid pt_len
```
Expected: `pt_len` should be >0 for each asset (typically 5,000-50,000 characters). If `pt_len` is null or 0, the Tenable export does not include plugin output — check the TA's export configuration. In Tenable.sc, navigate to Analysis > Assets > Export Settings and ensure "Include Plugin Output" is enabled. In Tenable.io, the export API must include `"filters": {"plugin_id": {"include": true}}`.

**EDR detection test:**
```
index=tenable sourcetype=tenable:sc:assets earliest=-1d
| eval edr_ok=if(match(plugins_text,"(?i)edr|defender|crowdstrike|sentinel|carbon.?black|sentinelone|cylance"),1,0)
| stats count(eval(edr_ok=1)) as edr_detected count(eval(edr_ok=0)) as edr_missing
```
Expected: `edr_detected` should be high (majority of assets have EDR). If `edr_detected=0` and you know EDR is deployed, the regex pattern may not match your EDR product's name in Tenable's plugin output. Investigate: `index=tenable sourcetype=tenable:sc:assets dns_name="known-edr-host" | table plugins_text` and search for your EDR vendor's name. Update the regex accordingly.

**MFA detection test:**
```
index=tenable sourcetype=tenable:sc:assets earliest=-1d
| eval mfa_ok=if(match(plugins_text,"(?i)mfa|okta|hello|yubi|duo|rsa.?securid|fido2|authenticator"),1,0)
| stats count(eval(mfa_ok=1)) as mfa_detected count(eval(mfa_ok=0)) as mfa_missing
```
Note: MFA detection via Tenable is inherently less reliable than EDR detection. MFA is often a cloud service (Okta, Duo, Entra ID) that doesn't leave a local footprint on the endpoint. If `mfa_detected` is very low, consider supplementing with Okta/Entra data (see Extended Search below).

**Lookup deployment:**
Place `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv` in the lookups directory. Define in `transforms.conf`:
```ini
[nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations]
filename = nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
```
Validate: `| inputlookup nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv | stats count dc(asset_fqdn) as assets`. Verify `assets` matches the expected in-scope population.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.28 NIS2 Cyber Hygiene Baseline Compliance`.

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **`inputlookup nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv` as the driver**: the lookup defines the universe of assets that must meet hygiene baselines. This is important because not every Tenable-scanned asset is in scope for NIS2 — only those supporting essential or important services.

• **`join asset_fqdn` (inner join)**: note this is an inner join, not left. This means assets in the lookup that have no Tenable data will disappear from the results. This is intentional — if Tenable hasn't scanned the asset, we can't assess its hygiene posture. However, this creates a blind spot: unscanned assets are invisible. Add a companion panel to the dashboard that shows unmatched lookup rows: `| inputlookup nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv | join asset_fqdn type=left [search index=tenable ...] | where isnull(mfa_ok)`.

• **Regex-based detection (`match(plugins_text, "(?i)...")`)**: the `(?i)` flag makes the match case-insensitive. The regex `mfa|okta|hello|yubi` looks for any mention of MFA-related products in the entire `plugins_text` blob. This is a broad heuristic:
  - `mfa` — catches generic references to multi-factor authentication in plugin descriptions
  - `okta` — catches Okta Verify agent installations
  - `hello` — catches Windows Hello for Business (but may false-positive on unrelated plugins mentioning "hello")
  - `yubi` — catches YubiKey references
  Similarly, `edr|defender|crowdstrike|sentinel` catches major EDR vendors. The regex can be externalized to `nis2_edr_markers.csv` for maintainability.

• **`hygiene_failures` as a bitmask count**: `if(mfa_ok==0,1,0) + if(edr_ok==0,1,0)` produces 0 (both present), 1 (one missing), or 2 (both missing). The `where hygiene_failures > 0` filter keeps only non-compliant assets. For dashboards, show the breakdown: `| eval missing_controls = case(mfa_ok==0 AND edr_ok==0, "MFA + EDR", mfa_ok==0, "MFA only", edr_ok==0, "EDR only")`.

• **`where hygiene_failures > 0`**: only surfaces non-compliant assets. For a compliance scorecard showing the fleet-wide picture, remove this filter and add: `| stats count(eval(hygiene_failures=0)) as compliant count(eval(hygiene_failures>0)) as non_compliant | eval compliance_pct=round(compliant/(compliant+non_compliant)*100,1)`.

Extended search — supplementing MFA with Okta enrollment data:
If your MFA is provided by Okta and not detectable via Tenable, append an Okta factor enrollment check:
```spl
| append [
    search index=auth sourcetype=okta:log eventType="user.mfa.factor.activate" earliest=-90d
    | stats dc(target{}.displayName) as factor_count by actor.alternateId
    | where factor_count > 0
    | rename actor.alternateId as owner_email
    | eval okta_mfa_enrolled=1
    | fields owner_email okta_mfa_enrolled
]
```
Then join on `owner_email` (from the hygiene expectations lookup) and update: `| eval mfa_ok=if(mfa_ok=1 OR okta_mfa_enrolled=1, 1, 0)`.

Schedule: cron `0 6 * * *` (daily at 06:00). Time range: `-1d to now`. Trigger: `Number of results > 0`.

Alert actions: (1) Email to the CISO office with the list of non-compliant assets and their `hygiene_failures` count. (2) For `hygiene_failures=2` (both controls missing): create a ServiceNow incident via the TA's alert action with assignment group `Endpoint-Security`, priority `P2`. (3) Write all results to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:hygiene:baseline` for trend tracking.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **EDR parity check**: select five assets where `edr_ok=0`. For each, verify in the EDR console (CrowdStrike Falcon > Hosts, or Microsoft Defender Security Center > Device inventory) whether the EDR agent is actually absent. If the EDR console shows the agent as installed, the Tenable plugin may not be detecting it — investigate the `plugins_text` for that host: `index=tenable sourcetype=tenable:sc:assets dns_name="<host>" | table plugins_text`. Search for the EDR vendor name. If absent, the Tenable scan may not include the relevant plugin — enable it in Tenable.sc > Scan Policy > Plugins.

(b) **MFA parity check**: select three assets where `mfa_ok=0`. Verify in the MFA provider's console (Okta > Directory > People, filtered by the asset's owner) whether MFA is enrolled. If MFA is enrolled in Okta but not detected in Tenable, this confirms the limitation of the Tenable-based approach — implement the extended search with Okta data.

(c) **Join cardinality check**: `| inputlookup nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv | stats count as lookup_count`. Compare with `index=tenable sourcetype=tenable:sc:assets earliest=-1d | stats dc(dns_name) as tenable_count`. If `tenable_count` < `lookup_count`, some in-scope assets are not being scanned by Tenable — this is a data gap that should be escalated separately.

(d) **Regex precision test**: create a synthetic `plugins_text` value containing "The security defender of the network" (not an EDR product). Run the regex `match("The security defender of the network", "(?i)edr|defender|crowdstrike|sentinel")`. This will match `defender` — a false positive. If false positives are problematic, tighten the regex to `(?i)\bdefender\s+(for\s+endpoint|atp|advanced)` or use the external markers CSV with more specific patterns.

(e) **Compliance percentage verification**: run the full search without the `where hygiene_failures > 0` filter. Calculate: `| stats count(eval(hygiene_failures=0)) as ok count(eval(hygiene_failures>0)) as fail | eval pct=round(ok/(ok+fail)*100,1)`. Compare this percentage with the CISO's expected fleet compliance. If significantly lower, investigate data quality issues before trusting the metric.

(f) **Mandatory control exemption test**: add a row to `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv` with `mandatory_mfa=0` (MFA exempt — e.g., an OT HMI panel). Modify the SPL to respect exemptions: `| eval mfa_ok=if(mandatory_mfa=0, 1, mfa_ok)`. Run the search. Verify the exempt asset does not appear as non-compliant.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Cyber Hygiene Baseline`):
• **Row 1 — Fleet scorecard**: Single-value `Fleet MFA compliance %` (green ≥ 95%, amber ≥ 80%, red < 80%). Single-value `Fleet EDR compliance %`. Single-value `Total non-compliant assets` (count). Single-value `Both controls missing` (count of `hygiene_failures=2` — highest priority).
• **Row 2 — Treemap**: treemap by `business_service` showing `hygiene_failures` count as size and compliance percentage as colour. This visualisation immediately shows which business services have the worst hygiene posture and the largest exposure.
• **Row 3 — Bar chart**: stacked bar of `missing_controls` categories (MFA only, EDR only, MFA + EDR) by `business_service`. Shows whether the problem is concentrated in MFA deployment, EDR deployment, or both.
• **Row 4 — Unscanned assets panel**: `| inputlookup nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv | join asset_fqdn type=left [search index=tenable sourcetype=tenable:sc:assets earliest=-7d | stats count by dns_name | rename dns_name as asset_fqdn] | where isnull(count) | table asset_fqdn business_service`. These are the blind spots — assets in scope but not scanned.
• **Row 5 — Trend**: `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:hygiene:baseline | timechart span=7d dc(asset_fqdn) as non_compliant`. Shows whether the non-compliant population is shrinking over time.

Runbook (owner: CISO / Endpoint Security):
1. Daily: review alert. Triage by `hygiene_failures`:
   - `hygiene_failures=2` (both missing): critical — these assets have no MFA and no EDR. They are the most vulnerable endpoints in the fleet. Immediate action: verify the asset exists and is in use. If yes, deploy EDR agent via SCCM/Intune and enable MFA in Okta/Entra. If no, decommission and remove from the lookup.
   - `hygiene_failures=1` (one missing): high — check which control is missing. EDR deployment is typically faster (push via MDM). MFA enablement may require user communication and enrollment.
2. Weekly: review the unscanned assets panel. For each asset not scanned in 7 days, verify the Tenable scan schedule includes the asset's network segment. If the asset is in a restricted OT zone, request a passive network monitoring scan instead.
3. Monthly: generate the compliance scorecard. Present to the CISO with trending compliance percentages. Archive with RFC3161 signing.
4. Quarterly: review the `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv` with the CISO. Update based on new asset deployments, decommissions, and changes to the mandatory control requirements.

ServiceNow integration: configure the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow alert action to POST non-compliant assets to `/api/now/import/u_hygiene_remediation_queue` with fields `asset_fqdn`, `business_service`, `missing_controls`, `priority` (P2 for both missing, P3 for one missing).

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Join timeout on large Tenable exports.** If `index=tenable` contains millions of events, the `join` may time out (default 60 seconds). Fix: precompute a summary index with `| collect index=summary_hygiene marker="daily_hygiene_snapshot"` running nightly. The main search then queries the summary instead of raw Tenable data: `search index=summary_hygiene marker="daily_hygiene_snapshot" earliest=-1d`.

• **False `edr_ok=1` due to generic regex match.** The regex `(?i)edr|defender|crowdstrike|sentinel` matches "Microsoft Defender SmartScreen" (a browser feature, not EDR) or "Azure Sentinel" (a SIEM, not endpoint EDR). Fix: tighten the regex to `(?i)(microsoft\s+defender\s+(for\s+endpoint|atp|advanced\s+threat))|(crowdstrike\s+falcon)|(sentinelone)|(carbon\s*black)|(cylance\s+protect)`. Alternatively, use the external `nis2_edr_markers.csv` lookup and iterate: `| lookup nis2_edr_markers.csv | mvexpand regex_pattern | eval edr_ok=if(match(plugins_text, regex_pattern),1,edr_ok)`.

• **EDR vendor renamed product string after acquisition.** CrowdStrike acquired Humio — if Tenable plugins report "Humio Agent" instead of "CrowdStrike Falcon", the regex misses it. Fix: maintain `nis2_edr_markers.csv` and add new product names as vendors rebrand. Subscribe to the Tenable plugin changelog to detect new detection plugins.

• **CSV stale — new assets not in the hygiene expectations lookup.** The `nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv` hasn't been updated since the last CMDB refresh. New servers deployed in the last quarter are in Tenable but not in the lookup, so they don't appear in the compliance check. Fix: automate CSV generation from the CMDB. Schedule a ServiceNow REST query weekly: `/api/now/table/cmdb_ci_server?sysparm_query=u_nis2_scope=true` and convert the result to CSV format. Monitor lookup freshness: `| inputlookup nis2_asset_hygiene_expectations.csv | stats max(_time) as last_update` (if the lookup has a timestamp column), or compare `dc(asset_fqdn)` against `index=tenable | stats dc(dns_name)`.

• **Zero Tenable events — API lockout.** The Tenable.sc API session expired or the API key was rotated. Fix: check `$SPLUNK_HOME/var/log/splunk/ta_tenable_*.log` for `401 Unauthorized` or `403 Forbidden` errors. Re-authenticate in the TA configuration. If using Tenable.io, regenerate the API access/secret key pair in Tenable.io > Settings > My Account > API Keys.

• **Hostname case mismatch between Tenable and lookup.** Tenable reports `SRV-DB-PROD.EXAMPLE.COM` while the lookup has `srv-db-prod.example.com`. The inner join silently drops these mismatches. Fix: add `| eval dns_name=lower(dns_name)` in the subsearch and `| eval asset_fqdn=lower(asset_fqdn)` after the `inputlookup`. Also ensure `case_sensitive_match = false` in `transforms.conf`.

• **Linux servers flagged for missing Windows Hello MFA.** The regex `(?i)mfa|okta|hello|yubi` matches "hello" in Tenable's SSH banner plugin output ("SSH-2.0-OpenSSH hello"), creating a false positive. But more critically, Linux servers that authenticate via SSH key don't use traditional MFA — they should be classified with `mandatory_mfa=0` in the hygiene expectations lookup, or the regex should be refined to exclude SSH-context matches: `(?i)\b(mfa|multi.?factor|okta\s+verify|windows\s+hello|yubikey|duo\s+security|fido2)\b`.
grandmaExplanation: We keep a checklist of the basic safety gadgets each important computer should have—like a second login step and protective software—and compare it to what our scanning tools actually see. When something is missing, we list it clearly for the people who fix machines.
